transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Teams to Zoom, how are you, Tyler?

I'm doing great.
Happy Friday.
Great to meet you.

Definitely very great to meet you as well.
I believe, I know I've checked out where you were, but remind me, where are you located?
Yeah, for sure.
I'm in Houston, Texas.
Ever been?
Yeah, I've been to Austin.

Texas is my current territory right now, so I learned a lot about, you know, the big four and overall, so, and been working Texas hours, so I'm very familiar, or very familiar, and then Austin, I've been there five, six times, so that was fun, Rainy Street and all that kind of stuff.
Oh, yeah, for sure.
Austin's such a fun place to go, and have you ever been out on the lake, like paddleboarding or anything like that?

Yeah, I love, love going to Austin, my little sister used to live out there, and went to UT, but I went to A&M, so we've got the sibling rivalry, I don't know if you're a football fan, but I've just been like, amazed with A&M this year, they're undefeated.
It's the best they've ever been since I've been alive.
But, yeah, I guess, you know, what do you like to do sort of outside of work?
Sports, your thing, or music?
I mean, sports.

then I like snowboard.

at all.

Well, good just like getting to know you a little bit and didn't want to jump right into like interview mode because at the end of the day, like, you know, I'm the founding AE here and the next salesperson we bring on, it's going to, you know, we're going to get creative, right?
We're going to get very collaborative.
There's a lot of unknowns that we're figuring out as we're doing our work.

Just, just talking to you.
I love that.
I feel that if working, who you're working with every day is so important in the whole process.
If one of the most important, I mean, like you said, me and you are going to spend it.
We would potentially be spending the majority of our time together, right?
More than our wife, or a lot of our time, a collab.
Yeah.
So, you know, already a lot of similarities.
So, so love, love the start.
Yeah, me too, man.
Well, I think the first thing I'd love to dig into, and I do have some questions that I have to ask as part of the process.
But, you know, I've dug into your background and like, I love the note that you sent over to the team.
Just tell me like a little bit about like how you got into software sales.
And then that time at Jasper, since you were like AE number two, would love to hear just like, what are some of the biggest things you learned having to actually go through that process, right?
Of like building from scratch.
So, yeah, just tell me a little bit more about those two things to start.
Yeah, no, those are, those are great questions.
Getting into software sales, I met a rep or I met someone who worked at, at, through a wedding at Jasper.
So I, and I was connected with the VP and through the connection.
And I was able to get an interview.
And through that, he really liked my just tenacity, hustle, my background.
And I went through, you know, kind of their initial interview, did a mock and what landed the role.
It was outside of what I normally sold.
So it was my first SaaS.
And he's really sold my potential.
then going into being the number two AE was going into probably a similar where you're at.
I had just been hired right after the founding AE, which was probably a month after the founding AE.
And they had only sold one deal for $50K for business level ACV.
So it was really building this out.
We had a lot like a high, like tons of prosumers.
We had $60 million in ARR.
Maybe, no, I think when I started $50 million in ARR of just prosumer and $50,000 in business revenue.
When I left, it was $20 million in prosumer and $30 million.
$32 in enterprise, $32 million.
So really was able to grow that.
But I just got an Excel list of going after these accounts.

So what I learned was a lot of trial and error process, understanding what marketers really care about, what are their KPIs, what the difference between above the line and below the line, like how to treat a user versus an actual buyer.

Like what are the KPIs for your CMO are going be much different than your KPIs for, you know, social media, a social media specialist versus like your content creation specialist, all of these different.
So it's really just this, this, this with AI on top of that, this massive acceleration of, of, of my knowledge base of enterprise selling.
And we did a lot of mid-market and SMB too.
So it was all over the place because we didn't have segmentation or territories.
This culmination of acceleration, rapid growth, learning, adding tools, picking between tools, what cadences work, what don't, what are the best practices.
It was really just a massive growth for the company and myself in this really compacted time.
Yeah, it makes a lot of sense.
And I'm sure it was like a very challenging, but also rewarding experience.

Because I know Jasper, yeah, it's interesting.
It has like, you know, the prosumer and then the enterprise.
And I did that at AIR, right, where like there was a pro plan and we tried to upsell to enterprise, but we created the enterprise plan after I joined.
So sort of what, I mean, I'm not like, you know, the answer to this question doesn't matter.
I'm just curious.
Yeah.
Exactly, right?
You're like, hey, do you want to play 10X that you're paying for a CS?
Well, you get a CS though.
So if you know that language, right?
Like what do we get?
That's not on the enterprise plan.
Well, you get an extra brand voice and you get a customer success manager, but we don't need one, but it's 10X.
So when I first started, my first deal was 15K.
And then when I left, it was in the 75 to 150 range.
So we grew a ton and we added solutions as a service.
So basically we're, we're coming in with our solutions architects and, and, and really breaking down their, their workflows and their processes and building like the custom creation process.
So that brought the ACV like massively up.
And so it was, I, and I worked with the ClickUps of the world to the pilot, pilot, if you're familiar with a pilot, pilot, flying J, they're like the.
Biggest travel center in the United States.
One of the top revenue companies, top 10 private before, I think it's Berkshire and Hathaway, purchased them.
And there's a customer's business case I was able to bring out of them, but I landed them sub six figure and then expanded them to post six figure.

So it was just all over the place when it came to, it was really understanding their process and consultative and really value-based selling on, I would charge $150,000 for one client and $30,000 for another.

But to be more, to have to put a number when I could be more in this, you know, $75,000 to $150,000 range, just depended on if I'm selling solution architects with that and custom workflows or how many users they had.
Do they have 75 users or do they have four users?
Makes a lot
And I feel like it's so important early stage to have creativity in like the pricing model so that, you know, one client's willingness to pay, might be closer to 30K, right?
And that makes sense for them.
But if you do have someone with the appetite to spend a lot as a seller and as a company, like you need a way to be able to execute.

So I feel like we could talk about it.

It was really about accelerating timelines, business level ROI, like direct.
I don't
Like three to five X of three to five X content creation, really easy to kind of put together, automate your marketing systems.
That's what really it would be like the pitch in different kind of specific scenarios, but automate your social media.
A lot about just automating that the marketing systems there and your processes is we went from more of just like create content faster to understanding your workflows.
And maybe if it was a sales team, it would be ABM marketing.

I would have an entire kind of pitch around ABM marketing from website scraping, LinkedIn, all that for a sales leader.
Or if it was a CMO, it would be campaigns, you know, from social media all the way to blogs and to white papers, like putting that all together.
And then a business case with, with what does that ROI look like for our, our, our employer, I mean, our.
It makes a lot of sense.

That's, I feel like, my just high-level impression of the platform.
But take me deeper than that, right?
Beyond just being able to create copy faster, what would Jasper unlock for a CMO?
Let's not do the sales team, but I do love the ABM example.
But for a CMO, yeah, what would that unlock for their team?
Yeah, so it would unlock the more strategic initiatives for the team.
it'd give time back to their team in a really scaled way for the team to focus on new ventures or have time back to really upscale that specific project.
So, for example, if we went into a social media campaign that normally would take like a month and a half, now we could get that automated too.
Within, you know, launch and first draft within hours, days.
And then it's about find the human touch now.
So AI automating and then getting the human touch and refining it and being well ahead of deadlines, right, for the CMO on the actual initiatives and their campaigns.
Makes perfect sense.

Now you can create 90 in the same amount of time or in social media.
And then we could put like a, that would be a $78,000 savings a year and here, and then 50,000 for your social media.
So we'd actually be able to put a monetary value on, on the time versus just, you're going to say five hours, right?
We had to actually be able to monetize that time.

So I worked with MyChampion, who's the director, head of content, and there's, I think, 75, 150 people under him.
And they needed to produce more content, and they were either going to have to hire out or figure out a way.
And they were saving three to five hours per user per week, and they only had 15 users, and we monetized that.
So like $160,000 in direct savings if they would have hired the equivalent headcount.
And that allowed to expansion from 15 to 75 users and brought that into like well under the six figures.
And so that was really like an example of...

Collaborating with the champion, going over what he's actually paying and how much content he needs, what headcount he would need to fulfill that content, those content needs, and then how Jasper saves that.
And then taking that business case and the ROI and being able to take that right into leadership for a quick upsell.
I love that.
Yeah.
Thanks for going in a lot more detail there.

I was part of, I have the, I believe I still have it on my Gmail somewhere, but we, and with AI, it's a lot easier now, right, to put together a business case.
It's kind of, it was, we were, we had a, we had a, a customer.

What's important?

And then post-ChatGBT, it went to dry as it can be because everyone had ChatGBT.

Exactly.

mentioned that you're doing a lot of outbound there.

mean, yeah, I mean, I'm sure it helped with that outbound campaign, right?

mean, we're the more we can show that we're a trusted expert, like you said, and they can trust us.
